pi_items.source: International 10-20 system convention: odd-numbered subscripts denote left hemisphere electrodes. Confirmed via Wikipedia (10-20 system), EMOTIV documentation, American Clinical Neurophysiology Society Guidelines, and OpenBCI Ultracortex Mark IV default mapping documentation.
International 10-20 system convention: even-numbered subscripts denote right hemisphere electrodes. Confirmed via same authoritative sources as above.

OpenBCI Cyton 8-channel default mapping: CH1=Fp1 (left frontopolar), CH3=C3 (left central), CH5=P7 (left posterior temporal, formerly T5), CH7=O1 (left occipital). Verified via OpenBCI Ultracortex Mark IV documentation and OpenBCI forum electrode mapping discussion. P7 is the updated 10-20 nomenclature for T5 (American EEG Society, 1994).
OpenBCI Cyton 8-channel default mapping: CH2=Fp2 (right frontopolar), CH4=C4 (right central), CH6=P8 (right posterior temporal, formerly T6), CH8=O2 (right occipital). Verified via same sources.

The OpenBCI GUI Filters dialog was opened during live testing (screenshot evidence: 11_filters_dialog.png). The BandPass tab shows editable Start (Hz) and Stop (Hz) fields. Default is 5-50 Hz. The value 1 Hz is within the valid range and is a standard lower bound for clinical alpha analysis (per IFCN guidelines). The agent must change the Start field from 5.0 to 1.0.

Same Filters dialog BandPass tab. The agent must change Stop from 50.0 to 40.0. 40 Hz is a standard clinical EEG upper bound that includes the full gamma onset while excluding high-frequency electrical noise. Confirmed editable in live testing.

The Filters dialog Notch tab shows options including '50 + 60 Hz', '50 Hz', and '60 Hz'. The default notch setting in the GUI is '50 + 60 Hz' (both active). The task requires setting it to 60 Hz only, which is the North American power line frequency standard. Confirmed via live testing screenshot.

This is a user-defined name the agent must type into the Networking widget's LSL stream name text field. The existing task 'stream_lsl_custom_name' confirms custom LSL stream names are supported (that task uses 'OpenBCI_Station_A'). The name itself is arbitrary — the value is correct as specified by the task creator.

Confirmed as a valid Networking widget data type. The existing task 'configure_dual_network_streams' uses BandPower as stream2_type. The widget dropdown during live testing showed available data types including TimeSeries, BandPower, FFT, EMG, Accel/Aux. BandPower is selectable from the Data Type dropdown in the Networking widget.

This is a task requirement, not a data property. Live testing confirmed the recording mechanism works: the warm-up session auto-recorded a 366MB file with 586K lines at 250 Hz sample rate. 15 seconds of recording at 250 Hz = 3750 samples, which would produce a file of approximately 1-2 MB. The minimum is achievable. The existing task 'record_synthetic_session' requires 10 seconds of recording successfully.

Live testing confirmed: (1) the path is writable by ga user — write test succeeded, (2) the stale file at this path was correctly cleaned by setup_task.sh, (3) the export_result.sh script checks this exact path for report existence and content. gnome-terminal is available for the agent to write files via terminal commands.

All 6 widget types confirmed available in the widget dropdown during live testing (screenshot 10_widget_dropdown_list.png). The dropdown shows: Time Series, FFT Plot, Accelerometer, Focus Widget, Networking, Band Plot (=Band Power), Head Plot, EMG, EMG-Joystick, Spectrogram, Packet Loss, Widget Template. NOTE: The Band Power widget appears as 'Band Plot' in the dropdown menu, but is referred to as 'Band Power' in settings files and task descriptions. The 6-panel layout was confirmed functional (screenshot 09_six_panel_layout_active.png).

privileged_info_summary: OpenBCI GUI v5.2.2 running a Synthetic Cyton 8-channel session at 250 Hz. The 6-panel layout is selected from the Layout button in the top toolbar (grid icon, bottom-left option in the 4x3 grid of layouts). The Band Power widget is listed as 'Band Plot' in the widget dropdown but is called 'Band Power' in settings and documentation. In OpenBCI GUI, channel colors follow a fixed palette: CH1=Grey, CH2=Purple, CH3=Light Blue, CH4=Green, CH5=Yellow, CH6=Orange, CH7=Red, CH8=Dark Red. Left hemisphere = odd channels (CH1/Fp1, CH3/C3, CH5/P7, CH7/O1); right hemisphere = even channels (CH2/Fp2, CH4/C4, CH6/P8, CH8/O2). Per-channel selection in Band Power widgets is done via small toggle buttons within each widget panel — channels use 0-indexed numbers internally (0-7) but display as 1-indexed (1-8). The Filters dialog is opened via the 'Filters' button in the toolbar; BandPass tab has editable Start/Stop Hz fields (default 5-50 Hz), Notch tab has frequency options. The Networking widget supports LSL protocol with configurable stream name text field and Data Type dropdown (options include TimeSeries, BandPower, FFT, EMG, Accel/Aux). Expert Mode is toggled via a button in the toolbar (stored in GuiWideSettings.json as expertMode: ON/OFF); when enabled, pressing 'm' saves a screenshot to ~/Documents/OpenBCI_GUI/Screenshots/ as an OpenBCI-*.jpg file. Settings are saved via Settings > Save in the toolbar, creating JSON files in ~/Documents/OpenBCI_GUI/Settings/. Recordings save to ~/Documents/OpenBCI_GUI/Recordings/OpenBCISession_<timestamp>/ as OpenBCI-RAW-<timestamp>.txt files. The report file should be written to /home/ga/Documents/asymmetry_station_report.txt and must mention left-hemisphere channels (CH1, CH3, CH5, CH7 or Fp1, C3, P7, O1), right-hemisphere channels (CH2, CH4, CH6, CH8 or Fp2, C4, P8, O2), filter settings (1-40 Hz bandpass, 60 Hz notch), and the LSL stream name 'Asymmetry_Monitor'.
